docs.title: Home
docs.text: About Us    **Transforming Data into Insightful Action**    Welcome to  **Infera Group LLC**, a data science and analytics consultancy founded by James Eells, PhD. We specialize in applying predictive modeling, econometrics, and data visualization to solve complex business problems and drive measurable impact.  👋 About James Eells    I'm a data science leader with over two decades of experience in financial services, higher education, and social impact domains. My work blends rigorous statistical modeling with practical business insight to help organizations make smarter, faster decisions.    I hold a PhD in Economics from Oklahoma State University and have led high-impact analytics initiatives at Ameriprise Financial, American Express, and Oklahoma State University. My approach emphasizes transparency, reproducibility, and alignment with stakeholder goals.  🛠️ Tools & Technologies    - **Languages**: Python, R, SQL, SAS     - **Visualization**: Power BI, Tableau, Shiny     - **Cloud & MLOps**: AWS (SageMaker, Glue, Athena), Azure     - **Modeling**: Regression (All Flavors), LSTM, Causal Inference, Time Series     - **Governance**: SR 11-7 Compliance, Model Risk Management    🔍 Services    At Infera Group, we help clients:    - Design and validate predictive models   - Measure program impact using causal inference   - Optimize marketing and service strategies   - Build interactive dashboards and data pipelines   - Modernize analytics ecosystems (e.g., cloud migration, MLOps)   - Ensure compliance with model governance standards (e.g., SR 11-7)    We believe small, agile teams can deliver big results—especially when analytics is embedded directly into decision-making.

docs.title: 📫 Contact
docs.text:
“The goal is not just to build models, but to build trust in the decisions they support.”

© 2025 Infera Group LLC

docs.title: 🧠 Case Studies
docs.text:
Strategic insights powered by data and delivered through rigor.

Explore by focus area:

  • 📈 Analytics
  • ⚖️ Compliance & Governance
  • 🤝 Customer Experience

“The goal is not just to build models, but to build trust in the decisions they support.” — James Eells, PhD

🔙 Return to Homepage

docs.title: 📈 Analytics Case Studies
docs.text: Optimizing decisions through rigorous modeling, measurement, and visualization.

docs.title: 🎓 OSU Entry-Level Placement Assessment (ELPA)
docs.text:
Client Context OSU’s legacy placement methods leaned heavily on standardized tests, creating inequities and excess remediation.

Approach Introduced the Predicted Grade Index (PGI) using regression models with high school GPA, class rank, and test scores. Embedded in enrollment workflows and recalibrated routinely.

Impact Transformed placement accuracy, assessing over 4,400 students annually with remediation rates below 5%. Adapted to students without test scores to extend equity.

docs.title: 📊 Key Driver Analysis for Program ROI Measurement
docs.text:
Client Context Post-financial crisis, 75 programs needed standardized ROI metrics to justify funding.

Approach Built panel data regression with advisor, client, and market controls. Defined “net flows” and branded as Key Driver Analysis (KDA).

Impact Enabled program comparisons and capital allocation based on empirical value. Became the core evaluation framework across lines of business.

docs.title: 💼 Measuring Wholesaler Impact on Financial Outcomes
docs.text:
Client Context Firm leadership lacked visibility into how wholesalers affected net flows and product sales.

Approach Created dynamic panel regression with DAGs and zero-inflated Poisson models. Reproduced in Python for cloud deployment.

Impact Attributed 22% of net flows to wholesaler engagement. Validated team ROI and guided resource allocation.

docs.title: 📊 Transforming Net Flow Reporting with Dashboards
docs.text:
Client Context Static reporting delayed insight generation and hindered exploratory analysis.

Approach Built Tableau, Power BI (with Sankey), and Plotly dashboards, integrating data dimensions for geography, advisor, and time.

Impact Reduced ad hoc analysis demand. Empowered real-time exploration and strategic decision-making across the enterprise.

🔙 Back to Case Study Index 🔙 Return to Homepage

docs.title: ⚖️ Compliance & Governance Case Studies
docs.text: Aligning analytics with risk, regulation, and responsible oversight.

docs.title: 🔍 Predictive Risk Modeling for Advisor Surveillance
docs.text:
Client Context Rule-based surveillance created high false positives and consumed analyst bandwidth.

Approach Modeled five risk behaviors using logistic regression. Created a General Risk Index based on probability × impact.

Impact Reduced false positives by 15%. Improved targeting and introduced a transparent, data-driven compliance layer.

docs.title: ☁️ Migrating Legacy Analytics to AWS Cloud
docs.text:
Client Context SAS-based ecosystem was unscalable and non-compliant with modern ML practices.

Approach Migrated datasets via Glue/Athena. Rebuilt models using SageMaker AutoML and scored through Batch Transform. Integrated fairness with SageMaker Clarify.

Impact Boosted scoring performance by 30%. Delivered modern MLOps compliance ahead of schedule and reduced operational costs.

docs.title: ✅ Achieving SR 11-7 Compliance for Model Governance
docs.text:
Client Context Federal Reserve review flagged non-compliance with SR 11-7 across model lifecycle.

Approach Led a 12-month remediation program with gated milestones, collaborative remediation, and embedded lifecycle uplift.

Impact Achieved full compliance on schedule. Raised governance maturity and restored regulator confidence.

🔙 Back to Case Study Index 🔙 Return to Homepage

docs.title: 🤝 Customer Experience Case Studies
docs.text: Enriching service delivery through intelligent prioritization and engagement.

docs.title: ⚖️ Real-Time Optimization of Dispute Resolution
docs.text:
Client Context Manual, high-cost billing disputes slowed resolution and harmed satisfaction.

Approach Built a real-time expert system using predictive modeling and economic logic. Deployed in SAS on mainframe for seamless call-center integration.

Impact Reduced resolution time by 90%, cut costs by 30%, and increased customer satisfaction across the board.

docs.title: 💬 Early NLP for Customer Service Prioritization
docs.text:
Client Context Rep notes contained valuable urgency cues but lacked scalable processing.

Approach Used keyword extraction and sentiment rules to create escalation indicators. Embedded into triage models.

Impact Enabled targeted routing of priority cases. Proved the value of unstructured data in service ops.

docs.title: 💡 Lifetime Value Modeling for Service Differentiation
docs.text:
Client Context Static segmentation limited proactive service across customers and merchants.

Approach Built predictive LTV and suppression risk models using transaction and cost data. Deployed for batch-based prioritization.

Impact Improved identification of high-value accounts and reduced churn by 6%. Lowered servicing costs by 7%.

docs.title: 📬 Optimizing Lead Generation for Financial Advisors
docs.text:
Client Context Over-reliance on massive mail campaigns strained budgets and ROI.

Approach Modeled response/conversion for RFI/RFC leads and ranked prospects by efficiency score.

Impact Met lead demand at half the original volume. Improved campaign cost-efficiency and advisor satisfaction.

docs.title: 🧪 Accelerating Marketing Insights with Multivariable Testing
docs.text:
Client Context Legacy testing lacked nuance and masked variable interactions.

Approach Used Plackett-Burman followed by Resolution IV factorial designs to uncover main and interaction effects.

Impact Streamlined testing and uncovered high-impact creatives. Established a scalable testing standard.

docs.title: 🎯 Advisor Focus List for Targeted Engagement
docs.text:
Client Context Scaling outreach demanded a more intelligent approach to advisor prioritization.

Approach Used LightGBM to model sales propensity. Created the Advisor Focus List with dual-level segmentation and evaluation via score matching.

Impact Focus advisors produced 40% more sales. Became cornerstone of wholesaler strategy.

docs.title: 📈 Modeling Optimal Contact Strategy Using LSTM
docs.text:
Client Context Leadership suspected contact sequence optimization could improve sales.

Approach Used LSTM to model time-dependent advisor responses. Included contact type and cadence features.

Impact Identified 5–8 contact sweet spot and flagged suboptimal sequences. Refined wholesaler outreach strategy.

docs.title: 🔄 Quantifying Advisor Response to Investment Recommendations
docs.text:
Client Context Research group sought behavioral validation for quarterly signals.

Approach Built time-series models aligning advisor trades/net flows to rec timelines. Modeled decay effects.

Impact Buy recommendations increased trading volume by 50%, sell by 30%. Validated research ROI and influence.

docs.title: 🧬 Validating Retirement Recommendations with Synthetic Data
docs.text:
Client Context Retiree data lacked diversity for stress-testing proprietary models.

Approach Used SMOTE and noise-injected sampling to generate 80M synthetic profiles. Ran benchmark and proprietary models across population.

Impact Confirmed model alignment with fiduciary goals. Strengthened go-to-market confidence and model fairness.

🔙 Back to Case Study Index 🔙 Return to Homepage

docs.title: Current Projects 🚧 Under Construction
docs.text: Here are some sample projects from a variety of data sciene and causal inference use cases. Most are contained in runnable jupyter notebooks using python or R. Future projects will include dashboard samples in Power BI, Tableau, Dash, and Shiny.

docs.title: Using H2O's AutoML Package in Python - A Demo
docs.text:
In [1]: Copied! 
import h2o
from h2o.automl import H2OAutoML

h2o.init()
import h2o from h2o.automl import H2OAutoML h2o.init()

  JVM stderr: /var/folders/g7/lfghdp8d7_374vfn3p7fx8x00000gp/T/tmpajwa_gvw/h2o_jeells_started_from_python.err

Please download and install the latest version from: https://h2o-release.s3.amazonaws.com/h2o/latest_stable.html
H2O_cluster_uptime: 02 secs H2O_cluster_timezone: America/Phoenix H2O_data_parsing_timezone: UTC H2O_cluster_version: 3.46.0.7 H2O_cluster_version_age: 4 months and 3 days H2O_cluster_name: H2O_from_python_jeells_mbmhph H2O_cluster_total_nodes: 1 H2O_cluster_free_memory: 7.984 Gb H2O_cluster_total_cores: 6 H2O_cluster_allowed_cores: 6 H2O_cluster_status: locked, healthy H2O_connection_url: http://127.0.0.1:54321 H2O_connection_proxy: {"http": null, "https": null} H2O_internal_security: False Python_version: 3.10.5 final

H2O has its own data loading and manipulaton tools. Since the target is binary, you have to inform H2O of that fact by using the asfactor() function. Otherwise, H2O will assume a numeric target is continuous and run rergressions instead of classifications. H2O can import many differet fule types and from many different platforms and the documentation for different use cases is excellent.

In [ ]: Copied! 
# Load the dataset
data = h2o.import_file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbeells/infera-group/main/docs/projects/data/CreditRiskDataset.csv")

train, test = data.split_frame(ratios=[.8], seed=1234)

x = train.columns
y = "dlq_2yrs"
x.remove(y)

train[y] = train[y].asfactor()
test[y] = test[y].asfactor()
# Load the dataset data = h2o.import_file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbeells/infera-group/main/docs/projects/data/CreditRiskDataset.csv") train, test = data.split_frame(ratios=[.8], seed=1234) x = train.columns y = "dlq_2yrs" x.remove(y) train[y] = train[y].asfactor() test[y] = test[y].asfactor() 
Parse progress: |████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| (done) 100%

Now the fun part! Training the models involves a couple of lines of code. The max_models=20 tells H2O to build 20 models in the AutoML run. There is another stopping parameter, max_runtime_secs, that specifies the maximimum run time. Both can be set and AutoMl will stop when either one is hit. The code here is minimal but AutoML has numerous optional parameters supporting a wide variety of use cases. It's worth noting that H2O a StackedEnsamnle algorithm by default. If ensamble overfitting is a concern, you can use the exclude_algos parameter and provide a list of exclusions (e.g., = ["StackedEnsamble", etc...]) Once the cell is run, depending on your resources, it would be a good time to step away for a coffee break!

In [3]: Copied! 
# Initialize and run AutoML
aml = H2OAutoML(max_models=20, seed=1234)
aml.train(x=x,y= y, training_frame=train)
# Initialize and run AutoML aml = H2OAutoML(max_models=20, seed=1234) aml.train(x=x,y= y, training_frame=train) 
AutoML progress: |███████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| (done) 100%

[tips]
Use `model.explain()` to inspect the model.
--
Use `h2o.display.toggle_user_tips()` to switch on/off this section.

H2O provides a mountain of output diagnostics for the champion model (StackedEnsambnle, as expected). You will notice that the Gains/Lift Tables use a peculiar 16-quantile grouping scheme (at least it's odd to those of us use to the decile scheme). This is the default in H2O and supposedly reflects a balance between granularity and interpretabily. Sure.

A much more concise and interesting summary output is the leaderboard. This provides a ranked listing of the models considered. It will usually be the case that the stacked ensemble models will be the top models. H2O AutoML produces two stacked models. One is based on all models trained (AllModels) and the top performing model from the family of algorithms considered (BestOfFamily). The third model would typically be the champion if StackedEnsembles were excluded. The cell below is run on the training sample and the following cell provides the leaderboard, diagnostics, and explainability metrics for the test sample.

lb = aml.leaderboard
lb.head(rows=lb.nrows)

Confusion Matrix Confusion matrix shows a predicted class vs an actual class. StackedEnsemble_AllModels_1_AutoML_1_20250731_105517 Confusion Matrix (Act/Pred) for max f1 @ threshold = 0.3433221686883272 0 1 Error Rate 0 1093.0 553.0 0.336 (553.0/1646.0) 1 212.0 1412.0 0.1305 (212.0/1624.0) Total 1305.0 1965.0 0.2339 (765.0/3270.0) Learning Curve Plot Learning curve plot shows the loss function/metric dependent on number of iterations or trees for tree-based algorithms. This plot can be useful for determining whether the model overfits.
Variable Importance The variable importance plot shows the relative importance of the most important variables in the model.
Variable Importance Heatmap Variable importance heatmap shows variable importance across multiple models. Some models in H2O return variable importance for one-hot (binary indicator) encoded versions of categorical columns (e.g. Deep Learning, XGBoost). In order for the variable importance of categorical columns to be compared across all model types we compute a summarization of the the variable importance across all one-hot encoded features and return a single variable importance for the original categorical feature. By default, the models and variables are ordered by their similarity.
Model Correlation This plot shows the correlation between the predictions of the models. For classification, frequency of identical predictions is used. By default, models are ordered by their similarity (as computed by hierarchical clustering). Interpretable models, such as GAM, GLM, and RuleFit are highlighted using red colored text.
SHAP Summary SHAP summary plot shows the contribution of the features for each instance (row of data). The sum of the feature contributions and the bias term is equal to the raw prediction of the model, i.e., prediction before applying inverse link function.
Partial Dependence Plots Partial dependence plot (PDP) gives a graphical depiction of the marginal effect of a variable on the response. The effect of a variable is measured in change in the mean response. PDP assumes independence between the feature for which is the PDP computed and the rest.

docs.title: Using H2O's AutoML Package in Python - A Demo¶
H2O AutoML: Simplifying Machine Learning¶
docs.text:
H2O-3 is a fast, open-source platform designed to make building machine learning models on large datasets both scalable and practical. Its in-memory architecture and distributed design make it well-suited for enterprise-grade predictive analytics.

In response to the growing gap between the demand for ML expertise and the available talent, H2O has built tools that make machine learning more accessible—especially for non-specialists. Their AutoML platform goes beyond a basic interface and actually automates the process of training and tuning a wide variety of models, making it a practical solution for both newcomers and experienced data scientists.

docs.title: Streamlining the ML Pipeline¶
docs.text:
H2O AutoML allows users to:

  • Automatically train and fine-tune many models within a defined time or model-count limit.

  • Skip manual hyperparameter tuning for tricky algorithms like deep neural networks.

  • Use a single, minimal interface—just point to your data and target column, then optionally set a time limit or model count.

I find it valuable because it saves me development time by using AutoML’s wrapper function; thus freeing me up to focus on other tasks. For example, many of my causal inference use cases require a highly accurate prediction function as part of a broader modeling exerise but I usually don't have the bandwidth to build and evaluate a bunch of ML models. H2O takes care of this for me in a transparent way so I can focus my attention on getting the causal model right.

docs.title: Built-In Explainability¶
docs.text: Understanding models is made simple with built-in explainability tools. Whether you're looking at individual models or entire groups (like the best-performing ensemble), AutoML provides visual explanations with just one function call.

docs.title: Flexible Interfaces¶
docs.text:
AutoML can be accessed through:

  • R and Python APIs using familiar parameters (x, y, training_frame, etc.)

  • H2O Wave’s Web UI for a code-free experience

docs.title: Requirements¶
docs.text: The H2O website contains a wealth of information about its architectue, data ingestion and manipulation, installation and user support documents, and videos. The following requirements are recommended for compatibility with H2O-3:

docs.title: Operating Systems:¶
docs.text:
• Windows 7 or later
  • OS X 10.9 or later
  • Ubuntu 12.04
  • RHEL/CentOS 6 or later

docs.title: Languages:¶
docs.text:
R and Python are not required to use H2O-3 unless you want to use H2O in those environments, but Java is always required (see below).

  • R version 3 or later
  • Python 3.6.x, 3.7.x, 3.8.x, 3.9.x, 3.10.x, 3.11.x

docs.title: Browser:¶
docs.text: An internet browser is required to use H2O-3’s web UI, Flow. Supported versions include the latest version of Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Internet Explorer.

docs.title: numpy:¶
docs.text: H2O-3 only supports numpy<2.

docs.title: Java Requirements:¶
docs.text: H2O-3 runs on Java. The 64-bit JDK is required to build H2O-3 or run H2O-3 tests. Only the 64-bit JRE is required to run the H2O-3 binary using either the command line, R, or Python packages.

docs.title: Data¶
docs.text:
The data used in this demo are from Kaggle and decribed as follows:

This dataset has been designed as a benchmark for AutoML and predictive modeling in the financial domain. It focuses on assessing credit risk by predicting whether a borrower will experience serious delinquency within two years. The data comprises a mix of financial metrics and personal attributes, which allow users to build and evaluate models for credit risk scoring.

It contains 10 features and a binary target indicating serious delinquency in the next 2 years (1=Yes, 0=No). The data has a Kaggle usability rating of 10, putting it amonth the best availalbe for download. The data set is not particularly large (11 columns, 16,714 rows) and leveraging VS Code's Data Wrangler extension, appears quite clean (no missing values, no notable outliers, etc.). The data is balanced with the target evenly split by 0s and 1s. Thus, the data are not representative of credit risk data found in the "wild" where event rates are well below 50 percent. Given the curator states "the data has been designed as a benchmark," I assume the data are synthetically generated. Nevertheless, based on my experience in credit card risk modeling, the selected feature distributions seem plausible. In short, good enough for a demo.

docs.title: AutoMl Demo¶
docs.text: After following the installation instructions for python, The following code imports H20, AutoML, and initializes the H20 cluster. The output provides details on the loading process.

docs.text: Causal Inference & Data Science Consultant Strategic Modeling for Measurable Impact SAS | Python | R | Power BI | Tableau | AWS | Azure

docs.title: Ameriprise Financial, Phoenix, AZ
docs.text:
Vice President, Data Science 2005 – 2024

  • Led cross-functional teams designing ML pipelines and measurement frameworks to assess program impact and operational performance.
  • Developed econometric models to quantify incremental lift in enterprise net flows, supporting strategic decision-making.
  • Created interactive Power BI dashboards to visualize fund flows and program outcomes, enhancing stakeholder understanding.
  • Served on enterprise-wide governance committees to ensure compliance with model risk standards.

Senior Director, Data Science

  • Designed and implemented key driver analysis models to evaluate over 100 internal programs, directly informing executive strategy.
  • Built Tableau tools for senior leaders to explore program performance and identify areas for improvement.
  • Led A/B testing and experimental design for marketing and compliance initiatives, improving targeting and reducing risk.
  • Mentored data scientists and facilitated internal learning forums to promote data literacy and innovation.

docs.title: American Express, Phoenix, AZ
docs.text:
Director, Econometrics 1997 – 2005

  • Developed predictive models and economic logic for customer service and risk operations.
  • Applied NLP techniques to extract insights from qualitative data, improving dispute resolution strategies.
  • Estimated customer lifetime value using time series forecasting and multivariate analysis.
